fuzzy_prd:
Hey team, we need to build out the operator contract toolkit we've been discussing. The main idea is that devs shouldn't have to hand-write all the repetitive Kubernetes boilerplate every time they set up a new operator — things like resource definitions, permission rules, event records, and test wiring. Basically, you describe your resources once and the library figures out the rest.

We already have some of the scaffolding from the last CRD generation spike (you can look at how we handled versioning and group naming back then), so try to stay consistent with those patterns rather than reinventing things.

The tricky parts are: making sure schema mappings handle all the edge cases around nullable fields and special Kubernetes types, getting the permission grouping logic right so we don't end up with duplicate rule entries, and making the in-process test harness actually route lifecycle events correctly to the right handlers.

Also — and this is something that came up in last week's retrospective — there's been confusion about what happens when someone looks up a resource that doesn't exist in the cache, or tries to generate a schema for a type the system doesn't understand. We need those failure paths to produce something consistent and actionable rather than just blowing up.

The test runner adapter needs to stay completely separate from the core logic, just like we agreed. Someone will validate everything against the standard test case files once it's wired up.

A couple of extra specifics from the questions that came back. On naming, metadata_name is just the plural resource name plus the API group with a dot in between. So if the resource is TestSpecEntity, the group is kubeops.test.dev, and the plural is testspecentitys, then the metadata name is testspecentitys.kubeops.test.dev. Pluralization stays intentionally dumb here: just lowercase the singular name and append an 's'. No special cases, so TestSpecEntity becomes testspecentitys, not testspecentities. This should stay aligned with the CRD versioning and group naming conventions we already set in the CRD identity and collection generation work, especially the same metadata_name = plural + group behavior and the same storage version assignment approach.

On events, the name needs to be deterministic from the event content, and for the standard test event in namespace 'test-ns', the expected name is MGODYZKPJ7QDVVCN2RUBXNOB6TD6XGCHDT46OMD5QG534WNV7LXIH3XZ4ISN746LUSVT57PH6KZCC7C62Z5QWYO2P4YJJHU6OJ2MRHY. If we're publishing an event and there is already a prior record, meaning existing_count is provided, then count becomes existing_count + 1, the UID carries over from the existing record as existing-uid, and we reuse that same deterministic name. If there is no prior record, then count is 1 and uid is null.

For leader election behavior, if the lease is missing and the write succeeds, we move to 'Leader' and leadership_changes=1. If the lease is missing but the write hits a conflict error, the state is 'Candidate'. Same result if there is a valid lease owned by someone else: 'Candidate'.

On the generated CRDs, the collection should have exactly 5 entries, ignored models stay out of it, and contains_ignored must be false. The five CRDs are AttributeVersionedEntity, TestClusterSpecEntity, TestSpecEntity, TestStatusEntity, and VersionedEntity. Each CRD has exactly one storage version. For VersionedEntity with versions v2, v2beta2, v1, v1beta1, v1alpha1, the storage version is v2. For AttributeVersionedEntity with versions v2 and v1, the storage version is v1. Everything else in that set only has one version, so that single version is also the storage version.

One more thing on the in-process harness so nobody overthinks the expected discovery counts: it should discover and report 21 entities, 1 controller, 1 finalizer, 2 validators, and 1 mutator.

And for output determinism, printer columns need to come out in this exact order: Age (.metadata.creationTimestamp, date, priority 0), Namespace (.metadata.namespace, string, priority 0), NormalInt (.spec.normalInt, integer, priority 1), OtherName (.spec.normalLong, integer, priority 0), NormalString (.spec.normalString, string, priority 0). Also, an intOrString field should output exactly type=null, properties_present=false, preserve_unknown=null, embedded_resource=null, int_or_string=true, item_type=null, item_preserve_unknown=null, item_embedded_resource=null.

trigger_keywords: status subresource detection
how do we know if a resource has a status subresource
what determines whether status_subresource is true or false
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A resource reports status_subresource=true only when its model has a distinct, separate status shape defined. Resources that only carry a spec shape (like namespaced_spec) report status_subresource=false.

trigger_keywords: nullable field rendering
how should null values be printed in output
what token is used when a schema field has no value
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a schema field has no applicable value (e.g., no format for a string type, no nullable flag on a non-nullable field), the output renders the literal string 'null' — not an empty string, not omitted, but explicitly 'null'.

trigger_keywords: unsupported schema type error
what happens with an invalid property type
how are unrecognized schema types handled
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When schema generation is attempted for an unsupported or unrecognized property type, the system must output exactly 'error=unsupported_schema_property_type' and nothing else. No stack traces or generic exceptions should surface.

trigger_keywords: serialized property name
wire name vs property name
how does the schema use configured attribute names
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The schema must use the configured serialized (wire) name from the property attribute, not the original source property name. When checking the output, contains_name_from_attribute must be true and contains_original_property_name must be false.

trigger_keywords: RBAC verb grouping
how are duplicate permission entries merged
when are RBAC rules combined into a single rule
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: RBAC rules with identical verb sets across different resources are merged into a single rule entry with all matching resource names listed together. This grouping is deterministic and alphabetically ordered within each group.

trigger_keywords: leader election lease rules
what RBAC rules does leader election require
when are lease resources included in RBAC output
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When leader election with leases is enabled, the generated RBAC rules include 'leases' grouped with other wildcard-verb resources, and also include deployment get/list and deployment/status get/patch/update rules. Without lease-based leader election, these rules are omitted.

trigger_keywords: cache missing resource error
what is returned when a resource is not in the cache
how does the cache report a lookup failure
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: When a resource UID that does not exist is looked up in the cache, the system outputs 'error=resource_not_found' followed by 'uid=<requested-uid>'. After a resource is removed, a subsequent lookup for the same UID must also return this error response.

trigger_keywords: cache comparison categories
what change types does the cache detect
how does the cache classify differences between two resource versions
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The cache detects and classifies differences between two resource versions into observable categories. A change in the status field is reported as 'StatusModified'; a change in the finalizers list is reported as 'FinalizersModified'. The comparison output includes the category and the resource UID.

trigger_keywords: reconcile vs finalize event routing
how does the harness route a reconcile event versus a finalize event
what counters change when a finalize event is dispatched
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: A reconcile event increments reconciled=1 and leaves status_modified=0, deleted=0, finalized=0. A finalize event increments finalized=1 and leaves reconciled=0, status_modified=0, deleted=0.

trigger_keywords: input/output adapter separation
how should JSON parsing be separated from business logic
where does the execution adapter boundary sit
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The JSON I/O adapter must be strictly separated from the core domain logic. The adapter is solely responsible for reading JSON commands and translating them into idiomatic method calls. Core business logic must never directly depend on stdin/stdout or JSON parsing — this separation is a hard architectural requirement.

trigger_keywords: how should I organize the code
single file or multiple files
project structure
directory layout
should I split into multiple files
monolithic or modular
how complex is the codebase
what files should I create
how to structure the implementation
should I separate business logic from IO
decouple core from IO
should business logic touch stdin stdout
how to separate parsing from logic
SOLID principles
separation of concerns
adapter pattern
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Scale-driven code organization: match physical structure to domain complexity. For micro-utilities or simple scripts a single well-organized file is fine. For systems with multiple distinct responsibilities (I/O routing, business rules, formatters, etc.) you MUST use a multi-file layout (e.g. src/, tests/) — never a single god file. Do not over-engineer simple problems, but strictly avoid monolithic files for complex domains. Strict separation of concerns is mandatory: core business logic must be completely decoupled from stdin/stdout and JSON parsing. The JSON test cases are a black-box contract for the execution adapter only — NOT the internal data model. Follow SOLID: SRP (separate parsing, routing, core execution, output formatting); OCP (core engine open for extension, closed for modification); DIP (high-level modules depend on abstractions, not I/O details). The public interface must be idiomatic. Errors must be modeled properly (typed exceptions or Result/Monad patterns), not generic faults.

trigger_keywords: do you have test cases
are there public test cases
what test cases are provided
can I see the test inputs
what features have test cases
show me the test cases
what test files exist
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Yes, public test cases are provided, one file per feature. Ask me for a specific feature by name and I will give you the full test case JSON.

trigger_keywords: what are the valid action field values
what values can action take
list all supported action values
what actions does the dispatcher handle
which action strings does the adapter support
what are the possible action inputs
tell me all the action names
what operations are supported
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The dispatcher routes on the 'action' field. Valid values are: 'get_missing', 'compare', 'remove'. Each value maps to one feature.

trigger_keywords: dotnet operator command dispatcher
cache nested action
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: The top-level dispatcher field is command. Valid command values include crd_identity, crd_generation_summary, crd_scalar_schema, crd_array_schema, crd_complex_array_schema, crd_extension_schema, crd_validation_metadata, crd_property_name, crd_printer_columns, crd_subresource, crd_invalid_type, rbac, event, operator_event, operator_registration, leader, cache, and test harness commands. The field action is nested and used only for command=cache with values get_missing, compare, and remove.

how does the dispatcher work
how is the program invoked
stdin stdout interface
execution adapter
how to read input
how does the test harness call my code
what is the entry point for testing
how does the runner call the program
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: You must implement an execution adapter (dispatcher) that is logically and ideally physically separate from the core domain. It reads a single JSON object from stdin, inspects the 'action' field to route to the correct core function, then prints the result to stdout matching the per-feature output contract. All policy durations in inputs are in seconds; all time points are epoch milliseconds. The adapter must not contain any business logic — every numeric result and boolean decision is delegated to the core domain.

trigger_keywords: how to write test.sh
test harness scaffold
automated test script
how should test.sh work
cases-dir parameter
where does test output go
output file naming format
how are test outputs named
rcb_tests structure
test scaffold deliverable
hidden_constraints.oracle_response: Deliver a bash test harness at rcb_tests/test.sh. It must accept --cases-dir <subdir> (default: test_cases) to point at a directory of *.json case files under rcb_tests/. For each case file it runs every case in the 'cases' array against the dispatcher, writing raw stdout (no PASS/FAIL metadata) to: rcb_tests/stdout/<cases-dir>/{filename_stem}@{case_index_zero_padded_3}.txt Example: rcb_tests/stdout/public_test_cases/feature1_next_retry_at@000.txt Output is namespaced by <cases-dir> so different directories never overwrite each other. A single entry point 'bash rcb_tests/test.sh' runs the full suite with the default dir.

context_pointers.actual_meaning: The existing scaffolding for CRD schema mapping including scalar, array, complex array, validation metadata, and Kubernetes extension schema features (Features 3.1 through 3.5), which define the patterns for nullable rendering and null literal output

oracle_data.fallback_response: That detail isn't something I have clear visibility on from the business side. I'd recommend checking the test case files under rcb_tests/public_test_cases/ and cross-referencing with the feature descriptions in start.md to confirm the exact expected behavior.

feature10_operator_test_harness.json.description: Provides an in-process operator harness that discovers registrations and routes resource lifecycle events to handlers.

feature1_crd_identity.json.description: Generates top-level Custom Resource Definition identity fields for a namespaced resource model.

cases.expected_output:
kind=CustomResourceDefinition
metadata_name=testspecentitys.kubeops.test.dev
resource_kind=TestSpecEntity
list_kind=TestSpecEntityList
singular=testspecentity
plural=testspecentitys
scope=Namespaced

feature2_crd_status_subresource.json.description: Reports whether a generated Custom Resource Definition exposes a status subresource based on the resource model shape.

feature3_1_scalar_schema.json.description: Maps scalar specification fields and nullable variants to OpenAPI schema type, format, and nullability.

cases.expected_output:
field=normalInt
type=integer
format=int32
nullable=null
field=nullableInt
type=integer
format=int32
nullable=true

field=normalString
type=string
format=null
nullable=null
field=nullableString
type=string
format=null
nullable=true

feature3_2_array_schema.json.description: Maps list-like specification fields to OpenAPI array schemas with item type and field nullability.

feature3_3_complex_array_schema.json.description: Maps arrays of structured specification objects to OpenAPI array schemas with object items and nested property schemas.

feature3_4_validation_metadata.json.description: Preserves validation and documentation metadata on generated OpenAPI schema properties.

feature3_6_schema_errors_and_names.json.description: Uses configured serialized property names in schemas and normalizes unsupported schema property types to a language-neutral error category.

feature4_printer_columns.json.description: Adds custom printer column definitions to generated Custom Resource Definition versions.

feature5_crd_collection_generation.json.description: Builds a Custom Resource Definition collection from registered models, excluding ignored models and assigning storage versions.

feature6_rbac_generation.json.description: Generates role rules from resource permissions, webhook requirements, leader-election settings, and shared verb groups.

feature7_resource_cache.json.description: Caches resources by unique identifier, reports observable update categories, removes resources, and normalizes missing-resource lookups.

feature8_event_publication.json.description: Publishes Kubernetes events directly or from deferred publishers, creating new event records or updating existing event counters.

feature9_leader_election.json.description: Checks a coordination lease and reports leadership state transitions for missing, self-held, expired, valid, and write-error scenarios.
